title: Child Care Workforce and Facilities Act of 2025
short_title: Child Care Workforce and Facilities Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill creates a grant program run by the Department of Health and Human Services to help areas that don't have enough child care options. The grants will go to states and tribal governments to pay for half the cost of training child care workers or building and fixing up child care centers. The goal is to make sure more families can find good child care in places where it's hard to find. States and tribes have to apply for the money and show how they will use it to help their communities.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Child Care Workforce and Facilities Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Child Care Workforce and Facilities Grants
sections.text:
This section explains what certain words mean in this law. An eligible child care provider is someone who provides child care and meets certain requirements set by an existing law. Indian Tribe, Tribal organization, and State have the same meanings as in another law called the Child Care and Development Block Grant Act of 1990.

A child care desert means an area where there are more than three times as many children under age five as there are child care slots available from licensed providers. It can also mean a community that the state or tribal government says has too few quality and affordable child care options.

A family child care provider is someone who provides child care in a home setting. A licensed family child care home is the place where a family child care provider works.

Portable credential means a qualification that a worker can use in different states or jobs. Stackable credential means a qualification that builds on other qualifications to help someone advance in their career. These definitions come from guidance issued by the Department of Labor in December 2010.

A postsecondary educational institution means a college or university as defined in the Higher Education Act of 1965. Secretary means the Secretary of Health and Human Services, who works with the Secretary of Education and the Secretary of Labor on this program.

Tribal area means a reservation or other area served by a tribal government. Tribal entity means an Indian Tribe or Tribal organization.

The Secretary of Health and Human Services will give grants to states and tribal governments through a competitive process. These grants help pay for projects that increase access to quality child care in states and tribal areas.

There are two types of grants. A child care workforce grant helps develop and expand the number of child care workers in child care deserts. A child care facility grant helps build, expand, or fix child care facilities in child care deserts, including family child care homes.

Each grant lasts for no more than five years.

To get a grant, a state or tribal government must submit an application with a plan. The application must include information about which state agency will manage the grant.

For workforce grants, the application must explain how the project will help more people get stackable and portable credentials in child care or early childhood education. It must describe outreach to people without college degrees about career paths in child care. It must explain how the project will help people complete education or training programs, including help with costs like tuition, books, and equipment.

The application must describe how the project will increase quality child care in child care deserts, make child care more affordable, and provide child care during nontraditional hours like evenings and weekends. It must explain how the project will help child care workers stay in their jobs and get better pay.

The application must describe how the state or tribal government will work with existing education and job training programs and use other available funding sources before using money from this grant.

For facility grants, the application must include similar information about increasing child care availability and must explain how the grant will expand family child care providers through building, expanding, or renovating facilities.

States and tribal governments can use workforce grant money to help people get education and training for child care careers. This includes paying for tuition, books, equipment, and creating training programs through child care resource organizations.

States and tribal governments can use facility grant money to build, expand, or renovate child care facilities. This includes paying for equipment, materials, and construction costs.

Up to ten percent of grant money can be used for administrative costs like managing the project.

The federal government pays fifty percent of project costs. States and tribal governments must pay the other fifty percent, which can be money or in-kind contributions like equipment or services. This non-federal share can come from donations from public or private groups.

The Secretary must evaluate how well the grants work. For workforce grants, this includes studying who benefits from the grants, how well people earn credentials, and whether child care workers are staying in their jobs and getting better pay. For facility grants, this includes counting how many facilities benefit from grants. The evaluation must look at the overall impact on reducing child care deserts across the country.

Within two years after the first grant period ends, the Secretary must report the evaluation findings to Congress.

It is United States policy that money from this program should add to, not replace, other federal and state funding for child care workforce development and facility construction.

sections.title: Section 3 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: Congress authorizes spending a total of one hundred million dollars to carry out this law for fiscal years 2025 through 2031.
